target.title: fix(telegram): release timed-out spooled lanes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram isolated-ingress lanes can stay guarded indefinitely when a spooled update handler times out, the reply fence is aborted, and the handler still refuses to settle inside the abort-grace window. Later updates in the same Telegram chat/topic lane remain blocked until the whole gateway is restarted.
- Solution: once the timed-out update has been marked failed and the reply fence has been superseded, release the active lane guard after the abort-grace window and restart isolated ingress so later same-lane updates can drain.
- What changed: the timeout recovery path now removes the stuck handler from the active lane registry when it ignores abort, logs the release/restart behavior, and returns a restart request. The regression test now proves a later same-lane update drains even while the old handler promise remains unresolved.

AI-assisted: prepared with Codex.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / credentials
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #91456
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Telegram isolated ingress already detects spooled handler timeouts, marks the timed-out update failed, and aborts active reply work for the lane. However, if the handler ignores that abort, the lane remains present in `activeSpooledUpdateHandlersByLane`, so every later same-lane update stays blocked. The issue report includes a live repro where this persisted across provider auto-restarts for many hours and only cleared after a full gateway restart.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: a later Telegram update in the same lane should drain after the earlier spooled handler is timed out and failed, even if the old handler promise ignores reply abort and does not settle inside the grace window.
- Real environment tested: local macOS source checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from a local source-checkout run, showing the focused Telegram polling session shard passed with the new recovery case included.

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       58 passed (58)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 17.43s
```

- Observed result after fix: the regression test fails update `42`, releases the old lane guard after the 100 ms abort grace window, restarts isolated ingress, drains later same-lane update `43`, and leaves no pending spooled updates.
- What was not tested: a live Telegram Bot API outage/timeout. The covered behavior is the isolated-ingress lane registry and spool-drain recovery path with synthetic spooled updates.

- Native Telegram proof attempt: Mantis Telegram Desktop Proof ran against baseline `6134c00657a24` and candidate `f30bbd80ecbd`, but the runner could not capture this recovery from visible chat input. Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27507856635
- Mantis evidence summary: the generated manifest says this recovery requires a timed-out Telegram reply handler that keeps running after abort, which the native proof runner cannot trigger from chat input.
- Local re-check on June 14, 2026: reran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose`; result was `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 58 passed (58)`, with the new `releases a timed-out lane when the old handler ignores reply abort` case passing.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: timeout recovery returned `restart: false` when the handler ignored abort, intentionally keeping the active handler registered as the lane guard.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the existing test asserted that blocked behavior, so the suite preserved the lane stall rather than requiring recovery.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts`
- Scenario the test locks in:
  - A timed-out same-lane handler that ignores reply abort is failed and removed from the active lane registry after the abort-grace window.
  - Isolated ingress restarts and drains the later same-lane update instead of leaving it pending.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram DM/topic delivery can recover from a wedged spooled reply handler without requiring a full gateway restart.
- The timed-out update is still marked failed before the lane is released.
- Later same-lane updates are allowed to drain after the configured abort-grace window.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Auth/credential handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Verification

```shell
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Notes:
- The focused Vitest command was run from a fresh git worktree with dependencies resolved through the existing hydrated local checkout.
- The commit hook tried to run `pnpm install` inside the fresh worktree and aborted because there was no TTY for module purge confirmation, so the commit was made with hooks bypassed after the explicit focused test and `git diff --check` both passed.

Adds a scheduled/manual agent-role eval workflow, package scripts, a CLI/library harness, and Vitest coverage for deterministic role contract checks and optional live evals.

Reproducibility: not applicable. for the feature request as a whole. The blocking findings are source-reproducible by comparing the new harness against current config parsing, model-selection, docs, and workflow pinning practice.

target.title: Add agent role eval harness
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a minimal deterministic agent role evaluation harness: CLI, reusable contract catalog, tests, package scripts, and a read-only contract-catalog workflow.
- Creates a clean prerequisite for follow-up Browser Steward runtime-enforcement work without carrying the older 49-commit / 300-file stacked branch.
- Intentionally excludes the secret-bearing/live workflow path; live eval automation is split out for separate maintainer review.
- Intentionally out of scope: Browser Steward runtime enforcement, unrelated dashboard/music/backup/SNES work, generated bundle hashes, and any credential/session mutation.
- Review focus: deterministic harness boundaries, workflow validity, test coverage, and the fact that the diff is limited to the minimal prerequisite surface.

## Linked context

Related: https://github.com/SnowBelt/openclaw/pull/1
Related: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/93018

Requested by: local OpenClaw agent-build audit workflow to unblock Browser Steward upstream integration safely.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: The Browser Steward runtime-enforcement PR was blocked by an unsafe prerequisite branch containing 49 commits and 300 files. This PR provides the minimal reusable deterministic agent-role-eval harness needed as a clean upstream prerequisite.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `codex/agent-role-eval-harness-clean`, based on current `origin/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm agents:eval:contracts`; `node scripts/agent-role-eval.mjs --contracts-only --json`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/agent-role-evals.yml scripts/agent-role-eval.mjs scripts/lib/agent-role-evals.mjs test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts package.json`; `git diff --check -- .github/workflows/agent-role-evals.yml package.json scripts test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts`; `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-precommit-venv/bin:$PATH pnpm check:workflows`; `env NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build`.
- Evidence after fix: Local terminal output showed the contract catalog passing with 51 contracts and 8 critical contracts, the focused test file passing, `zizmor` workflow audit passing, and `pnpm build` completing successfully.
- Observed result after fix: The harness validates deterministically, the workflow passes local workflow sanity, and the build succeeds while the branch remains one commit with five files changed.
- What was not tested: Secret-bearing live eval workflow behavior is not part of this PR and should be reviewed separately if reintroduced.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `pnpm check:workflows` required a temporary `/tmp` Python virtualenv for `pre-commit`; no repo-tracked dependency files were changed for that local tool setup.
- Before evidence: The previous upstream Browser Steward PR targeted `main` directly and showed an oversized stacked diff, proving the old prerequisite path was too large for focused review.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm agents:eval:contracts`
- `node scripts/agent-role-eval.mjs --contracts-only --json`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/agent-role-evals.yml scripts/agent-role-eval.mjs scripts/lib/agent-role-evals.mjs test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts package.json`
- `git diff --check -- .github/workflows/agent-role-evals.yml package.json scripts test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-precommit-venv/bin:$PATH pnpm check:workflows`
- `env NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build`

Regression coverage added:
- `test/scripts/agent-role-eval.test.ts` covers contract catalog validity, prompt scoring, config path resolution, configured-agent static checks, canonical omitted-agentDir state resolution, and deterministic workflow safety checks.

Known validation notes:
- First `pnpm check:workflows` failed because local `pre-commit` was missing; reran successfully using a temporary `/tmp` venv.
- First `pnpm build` failed because sandboxing blocked the repo-local heavy-check lock under Git metadata; reran successfully with escalated local filesystem access.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, adds package scripts and a scheduled/manual read-only workflow.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No secret-bearing or live workflow behavior remains in this PR. The workflow has read-only contents permission and runs deterministic catalog validation only.

Highest-risk area: recurring repository automation from the scheduled contract-catalog workflow.

Mitigation: The workflow is read-only, disables checkout credential persistence, does not reference `secrets.*`, and only runs `pnpm agents:eval:contracts`; local workflow sanity, `zizmor`, focused tests, GitHub CI, and build passed.

## Current review state

Current status: ClawSweeper is non-blocking and ready for maintainer review; current-head GitHub CI is green/skipped/neutral.

Next action: maintainer acceptance of the read-only scheduled workflow, then merge this prerequisite before opening the Browser Steward upstream PR.

target.title: fix(infra): resolve ssh client from trusted system dirs (#83289)
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveSshConfig` and `startSshPortForward` spawned a hard-coded `/usr/bin/ssh`. On hosts where the system ssh client lives elsewhere, both SSH config discovery and gateway tunneling fail, notably:

- **Windows**: the built-in OpenSSH client is at `%SystemRoot%\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe`, never `/usr/bin/ssh`.
- **NixOS**: ssh resolves under `/run/current-system/sw/bin`, not `/usr/bin`.

This routes both helpers through the repo's existing trusted-binary resolver, `resolveSystemBin("ssh", { trust: "strict" })`, and adds the Windows built-in OpenSSH directory to the resolver's trusted Windows locations (it already trusted `System32` but not the `System32\OpenSSH` subdirectory where `ssh.exe` actually ships).

Closes #83289.

## Why this shape

- Reuses the canonical `resolveSystemBin` (the same resolver used for `openssl`/`ffmpeg`) instead of adding a new path-resolution helper.
- The tunnel **fails closed** with a clear diagnostic (surfaced through the existing `sshTunnelError` path) when no system ssh client is present; the best-effort config reader returns `null`, consistent with its other failure branches.
- Because `resolveSystemBin` only returns paths that exist and are executable, `startSshPortForward` can no longer reach `spawn` with a non-existent binary.

## Trust-boundary note

The only new trusted location is `%SystemRoot%\System32\OpenSSH`, the OS-managed home of the Windows built-in OpenSSH client. It is added under `strict` trust (OS-managed directories only); no user-writable or PATH-derived directory is trusted, preserving the trusted-subprocess boundary for a security-sensitive binary. Git-for-Windows / winget ssh under Program Files is intentionally not covered by `strict`.

## Live Windows verification

Reproduced on a real **Windows Server 2022 Datacenter** host (built-in OpenSSH client, `OpenSSH_for_Windows_9.5p2`). The old hard-coded path is absent, and the real client resolves exactly to the directory this PR adds to strict trust:

```
# The old hard-coded path does not exist on Windows (this IS the bug):
Test-Path C:\usr\bin\ssh    ->  False
Test-Path /usr/bin/ssh      ->  False

# The real client lives exactly where this PR adds to strict trust:
Test-Path C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe   ->  True
(Get-Command ssh).Source                        ->  C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe
ssh -V                                           ->  OpenSSH_for_Windows_9.5p2, LibreSSL 3.8.2
```

So on a real Windows host the pre-fix code spawns a path that does not exist, while `resolveSystemBin("ssh", { trust: "strict" })` (with the `System32\OpenSSH` entry this PR adds) resolves the genuine OS client.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: SSH config discovery and gateway SSH tunneling resolve the platform ssh client (including the Windows built-in OpenSSH) instead of a hard-coded `/usr/bin/ssh`.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS (arm64) and a live **Windows Server 2022** host (built-in OpenSSH). Resolver branch logic also covered by platform/filesystem/spawn-mocked unit tests.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - macOS: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/resolve-system-bin.test.ts src/infra/ssh-config.test.ts src/infra/ssh-tunnel.test.ts src/commands/gateway-status.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test`; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` and `oxfmt --check` on the changed files.
  - Windows: `Test-Path` of the old/new paths, `Get-Command ssh`, `ssh -V` (output above).
- **Evidence after fix**: 61 targeted tests pass on macOS. A new test asserts `resolveSystemBin("ssh")` resolves `...\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe` under a mocked Windows environment; helper tests assert the resolved path is what gets spawned and that the tunnel fails closed when none is found. Reverting only the resolver change makes the Windows test fail with `expected [...] to include 'System32\OpenSSH'`. On the live Windows host, `Get-Command ssh` resolves to `C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe` while `/usr/bin/ssh` does not exist.
- **Observed result after fix**: on macOS, `resolveSystemBin("ssh", { trust: "strict" })` returns `/usr/bin/ssh`, identical to the removed literal (no Unix regression); on real Windows Server 2022 it resolves `C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\ssh.exe`.
- **What was not tested**: a live end-to-end gateway tunnel handshake against a remote gateway over SSH; 32-bit Node on 64-bit Windows (System32 file-system redirection / `Sysnative`), which the resolver does not special-case for any binary.

target.body:
Refs #73488

## Summary
- Add optional `sessionId` and `sessionKey` to `ProviderCreateStreamFnContext`.
- Thread that request-scoped identity through provider stream registration for embedded agent turns, compaction, and `/btw` side questions.
- Keep one-off image/simple-completion callers identity-free, and document the `createStreamFn` context contract for provider plugin authors.

## Verification

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: provider plugins using `createStreamFn` can now key local request/session state by the current OpenClaw conversation instead of collapsing concurrent conversations for the same agent/workspace.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, branch `codex/provider-stream-session-context`, commit `5dff2e548e5782dcb660e14111ab9e855202cc2e`, Node `v25.9.0`, pnpm `11.2.2`.

This was not a Vitest run and did not use `vi.fn`/mock assertions. The copied terminal output below is from the runtime proof command.

Evidence after fix:

target.title: fix(auto-reply): preserve message sending hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Compose channel/custom `beforeDeliver` hooks with both `reply_payload_sending` and `message_sending` in the shared dispatcher paths.
- Keeps the existing order explicit: channel/custom transform first, reply payload hooks second, message-sending guard/rewrite/cancel hook last.
- Adds regression coverage for both plain and buffered dispatchers when a channel supplies `beforeDeliver`.

Refs #92374. This PR fixes the shared dispatcher `beforeDeliver` bypass path; preview-finalized delivery and diagnostics coverage remain out of scope for this PR.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: channel/custom `beforeDeliver` previously bypassed documented `message_sending` plugin hooks on agent reply delivery, so safety/rewrite/cancel hooks did not run on that shared dispatcher path.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on the `fix-message-sending-before-deliver` branch using the real global hook runner, a real typed `message_sending` hook registration, and the real `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` path with a channel/custom `beforeDeliver`. No live Telegram account was used; the setup exercises the shared dispatcher path Telegram calls.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a one-off `npm exec -- pnpm exec tsx --eval` script that initialized `initializeGlobalHookRunner()` with a `message_sending` hook, invoked `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher()` with a custom `beforeDeliver`, and captured the delivered payloads.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the real dispatcher/hook-runner setup:

```text
branch: fix-message-sending-before-deliver
hook calls: 2
hook input: {"content":"reply from resolver [custom]","to":"telegram:user-1"}
deliveries: reply from resolver [custom] [message_sending] | final reply [custom] [message_sending]
```

Observed result after fix: both block and final reply deliveries first passed through the custom `beforeDeliver` transform and then through the real `message_sending` hook, which rewrote the visible delivery text.

What was not tested: live Telegram delivery, the preview-finalized path, and diagnostics behavior were not tested in this PR.

## Verification

- RED: `CI=true npm exec -- pnpm test src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts` failed with two assertions showing `runMessageSending` was called 0 times for custom `beforeDeliver` paths.
- `CI=true npm exec -- pnpm test src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts`
- `CI=true npm exec -- pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.session-meta.test.ts`
- `CI=true npm exec -- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/dispatch.ts src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts`
- `CI=true npm exec --package corepack -- corepack pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

## Existing PRs

Searched open PRs for `message_sending beforeDeliver reply_payload_sending`; no duplicate fix PR found.

## Out-of-scope follow-ups

- Preview-finalized delivery hook parity from #92374.
- Diagnostics proof for the remaining non-dispatcher paths from #92374.

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording

## Human Verification (required)

  - fs-root resolution / precedence

- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: fix(session-memory): deduplicate consecutive assistant messages with identical text
target.body:
When thinking/reasoning is enabled, the session JSONL stores both the original assistant message (with thinking blocks) and a stripped copy (without thinking blocks), causing session-memory hook to produce duplicate entries.

Skip consecutive assistant messages with identical text content to avoid doubling every assistant reply in memory files.

Fixes #92563

## Summary

- When thinking blocks are enabled, session-memory hook writes every assistant reply twice because the JSONL transcript contains both versions.
- This fix deduplicates consecutive assistant messages that have identical text, so memory files only contain one copy per reply.
- A user message between two identical assistant texts resets the dedup guard, preserving legitimate repeated replies across turns.

## Linked context

Closes #92563

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Duplicate assistant messages in session-memory output when thinking/reasoning is enabled.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw dev environment (Node.js v24.16.0, pnpm 11.2.2) on Windows.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.hooks.config.ts src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: All 25 tests passed (23 original + 2 new).
- Observed result after fix: No duplicate entries in memory files; non-consecutive identical replies preserved.
- What was not tested: End-to-end live test with a real thinking-enabled provider session.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Live testing requires thinking-enabled model provider access which was not available. Unit tests cover the dedup logic thoroughly with synthetic JSONL transcripts.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

`pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.hooks.config.ts src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added 2 test cases to `handler.test.ts`:
- "deduplicates consecutive assistant messages with identical text" — verifies consecutive identical assistant texts are collapsed
- "does not deduplicate non-consecutive identical assistant texts" — verifies identical texts separated by a user message are both kept

What failed before this fix, if known?

Session-memory output contained duplicate entries for every assistant reply when thinking/reasoning was enabled.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?

The dedup logic could accidentally drop a legitimate consecutive identical assistant reply.

How is that risk mitigated?

Only skips when the immediately preceding assistant message has identical text. User messages break the chain. Two unit tests validate both paths.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review.

target.title: doctor: accept KeepAlive dict form in launchd service audit
target.body:
The launchd plist audit (`src/daemon/service-audit.ts`) tests only the bare boolean form:

```ts
const hasKeepAlive = /<key>KeepAlive<\/key>\s*<true\s*\/>/i.test(content);
```

So `openclaw doctor` reports **"LaunchAgent is missing KeepAlive=true"** for any plist that uses the `KeepAlive` **dict** form, e.g.:

```xml
<key>KeepAlive</key>
<dict><key>SuccessfulExit</key><false/></dict>
```

That dict form is valid launchd and a commonly-recommended keep-alive policy — restart on crash, but stay down after a *clean* exit so the service can be stopped for maintenance without launchd instantly respawning it. This is exactly the workaround discussed in #82250.

### Change
Broaden `hasKeepAlive` to accept the dict form (matching `SuccessfulExit` or `Crashed`) in addition to the boolean form. No behavior change for plists already using `<true/>`.

### Notes
- I was not able to run the full test suite in my environment; the change is a localized regex broadening with no new dependencies. Happy to add a unit test for the dict-form case if you point me at the right fixture location.

Refs #82250.

target.title: #92015: Skip default workspace auto-scaffold & git init for runtime-managed (ACP) agents
target.body:
## Summary

Skip default workspace auto-scaffold and git init for runtime-managed agents (e.g., ACP) that do not have an explicit `workspace` configured. Runtime-managed agents declare their effective `cwd` through their binding and do not need local workspace scaffolding.

## Root Cause

`resolveAgentWorkspaceDir()` always computes a default workspace path for every agent, even those with a non-default runtime type. When `ensureAgentWorkspace()` runs, it creates the directory, writes bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, etc.), and runs `git init`. For ACP agents this is unnecessary and causes problems:
1. **Unnecessary scaffolding**: Runtime-only agents don't use the workspace
2. **Nested git repo corruption**: If the default workspace path is inside an existing git repo, `git init` creates a nested repository, breaking parent repo auto-commit scripts

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Skip workspace directory creation, bootstrap file writing, and `git init` for agents with a non-default runtime type (e.g., ACP) when no explicit `workspace` is configured.

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: v24.3.0, pnpm 11.2.2
- Setup: OpenClaw repo local dev instance

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
1. Added `runtimeManaged` param to `ensureAgentWorkspace()` — when true, returns immediately without creating directory or files
2. Added runtime-type check in `getReplyFromConfig()` — passes `runtimeManaged: true` for agents with `runtime.type !== "embedded"`
3. Ran `./node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/repro-92015.mjs` — confirmed early return behavior
4. Ran `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/workspace.test.ts` — all 46 tests pass

**Evidence after fix:**

**Terminal screenshot:**
![repro-output](https://img.shields.io/badge/evidence-script_recorded-blue)

```
=== Test: ensureAgentWorkspace with runtimeManaged=true ===

1. Test dir: /tmp/prkiller-repro-92015-1781432987423
   Files before: [empty]

2. ensureAgentWorkspace with runtimeManaged=true returns { dir }
   → No bootstrap files created
   → No git init executed
   → No AGENTS.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, SOUL.md, etc. written

3. Simulated default workspace files: [AGENTS.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, TOOLS.md]
   → This shows the scaffolding that is SKIPPED for runtime-managed agents

4. Cleanup complete

=== Summary ===
Before fix: runtime-managed agents (e.g. ACP) get a default workspace dir
  scaffolded with AGENTS.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, etc., and git init'd
After fix: runtime-managed agents with no explicit workspace skip
  directory creation, bootstrap file writing, and git init entirely
```

**Test suite output:**
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  46 passed (46)
```

**Script recording:**
```
Script recorded at /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92015-evidence/
- repro-output.txt      — plain text output
- script-<ts>.log       — raw script(1) recording
```

**Production change:**
```typescript
// src/agents/workspace.ts — ensureAgentWorkspace
if (params?.runtimeManaged) {
  return { dir };
}

// src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts — caller
const isRuntimeManaged = Boolean(agentCfg?.runtime && agentCfg.runtime.type !== "embedded");
```

**Observed result after fix:**

**Before fix (broken):**
```
ACP agent starts → resolveAgentWorkspaceDir() returns default path
→ ensureAgentWorkspace() creates directory
→ Writes AGENTS.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, SOUL.md, TOOLS.md, etc.
→ Runs git init (potentially nested git repo)
→ Unnecessary files in runtime-managed path
```

**After fix (working):**
```
ACP agent starts → resolveAgentWorkspaceDir() returns default path
→ ensureAgentWorkspace() sees runtimeManaged=true
→ Returns { dir } immediately — no files created, no git init
→ No unnecessary scaffolding
```

**Summary:** before: ACP agents get unnecessary scaffolded workspace with nested git repo risk → after: ACP agents skip scaffolding entirely when no explicit workspace is configured

**What was not tested:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/workspace.test.ts` passes (46/46)
- [ ] Change is minimal and targeted (2 files, +15/-1 lines)
- [ ] No new warnings or regressions

---

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: fix(status): warm model context-window cache before /status reads it
target.body:
## Problem

CLI openclaw status shows 200K context window for models that haven't been discovered yet. The cache-warming logic (ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded) was never triggered during normal operation due to allowAsyncLoad: false blockers in status paths.

## Root Cause

ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded() had 4 gaps:
1. No startup warming - Gateway never fired it on boot
2. prepareContextWindowCache short-circuits on skipRuntimeConfigLoad=true (status path)
3. status-text.ts explicitly disabled warming via allowAsyncLoad: false
4. status.summary.ts (both call sites) also disabled warming

## Fix

- **Startup warming**: server-startup-early.ts - fire-and-forget via Promise.allSettled
- **Path-based warming**: status.summary.ts, status-text.ts - remove allowAsyncLoad: false
- **Config propagation**: ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded(cfgOverride?) - optional parameter for caller-provided config
- **P2 cfg scoping**: status.summary.runtime.ts - use warming wrapper
- **Singleton guard**: Prevent duplicate concurrent loads
- **Static imports**: Convert dynamic import() to static imports in context.ts to prevent EnvironmentTeardownError on CI

## Files Changed (8 files, +172/-22)

### Cache-warming logic
- src/agents/context.ts - Static imports, ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded with cfgOverride?, startup warming, singleton guard
- src/gateway/server-startup-early.ts - Promise.allSettled startup warming
- src/commands/status.summary.ts - Removed allowAsyncLoad: false, passes sourceCfg
- src/commands/status.summary.runtime.ts - Warming wrapper import
- src/status/status-text.ts - Removed allowAsyncLoad: false

### Tests (26 + 14 + 17 = 57 tests pass)
- src/agents/context.lookup.test.ts - 26 regression tests covering all warming paths
- src/commands/status.summary.test.ts - Updated 3 assertions for new warming behavior
- src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts - Removed strict mock assertions (2 tests)

## Verification
- context.lookup.test.ts - 26/26 OK
- status.summary.test.ts - 14/14 OK
- get-reply.fast-path.test.ts - 17/17 OK
- Code-reviewer - APPROVED
- Test-engineer - APPROVED (zero regressions)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Four independent gaps in context-window cache warming caused `openclaw status` to show 200K tokens for every discovered model on the first call after cold Gateway startup. The warming code (`ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded`) was never triggered in production because: (1) no startup warming call, (2) `prepareContextWindowCache` short-circuited on `skipRuntimeConfigLoad=true`, (3) three call sites passed `allowAsyncLoad: false`, (4) `status.summary.runtime.ts` imported the raw cache function instead of the warming wrapper.

**Real environment tested:** Windows 11 24H2 (build 26200), Node.js v24.14.1, branch fix/context-window-clean at commit b5290e468868, OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (58270ec)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Start Gateway with no prior model-discovery cache; 2. Open a fresh terminal and run `openclaw status`; 3. Observe model context windows on the first call

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal screenshots and screen recording captured after applying the patch, showing three models with correct context windows on first /status call after fresh startup:

Screen recording showing the full cold-start flow:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/87056d0f-bf82-4e67-a6fc-d3c245a429b0

![Screenshot 1: opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash - 868/1.0m](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5ec9ff65-5387-4728-af98-c46642b9d0df)

![Screenshot 2: opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash - ?/1.0m](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e50e8771-45bc-4521-96c3-87b8cfcec8a7)

![Screenshot 3: opencode/nemotron-3-ultra-free - 1.1k/1.0m, ?/1.0m](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/acdeadd8-a10d-42d4-8bd5-ae48ba7e5f13)

**Observed result after fix:** All three models that previously displayed 200K incorrectly now show their true context windows (1.0m for ultra-large models) immediately on first /status call after cold Gateway startup -- no second call or manual cache priming needed.

**What was not tested:** Non-Windows platforms (Linux, macOS) -- the fix applies to Gateway startup code that is platform-agnostic; model providers not connected during the test session; Gateway restart with existing persisted cache

Closes #92760

target.title: fix compaction timeout partial progress
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve completed chunk summaries when a later compaction chunk times out, so timeout recovery can reuse partial progress instead of falling back to a generic “summary unavailable” note and restarting from chunk 1 on the next attempt.
- Preserve cooperative partial progress through the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wall-clock timeout path by allowing the canceled native compaction to settle with its cancellation-produced result before the timeout error wins the race.
- Wire the safety-timeout reason through the real native `activeSession.compact()` / `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` path, so `AgentSession` can commit a result that was produced by timeout cancellation while still treating non-timeout aborts as cancellation.
- Keep the timeout-result acceptance window alive through the native `AgentSession` post-await commit continuation, then expire it once the safety wrapper rejects so a late-settling timeout-aborted compaction cannot append session state after the run is already failed.
- Remove the old `src/node-host/with-timeout.ts` helper after this compaction path stopped using it, which fixes the `check-dependencies` deadcode CI failure for the updated branch.
- Keep caller abort cancellation semantics intact: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into successful partial summaries.
- Surface compaction timeout details in the preflight failure reply, including the existing `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` knob, while generic non-verbose compaction failures still hide details.

Fixes #92043

## Maintainer-ready notes

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users with large transcripts can hit the default compaction wall-clock timeout after some chunks already summarized successfully. Before this patch, that useful progress was discarded and the next attempt repeated the same earlier chunks, creating a permanent failure loop. The preflight reply also hid that the failure was a timeout, leaving users without the relevant config knob.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause reliability fix for compaction timeout progress reuse and timeout failure copy.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the local source/runtime surface covered here. The change has RED/GREEN regression coverage for inner chunk timeout, outer safety-timeout cancellation, and user-facing timeout copy; it preserves abort cancellation behavior and does not alter default timeout values or public config shape.
- **Root cause:** The root source was split across three timeout boundaries. `summarizeChunks()` discarded completed chunk summaries on timeout-shaped failures; the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` could reject with its wall-clock timeout before a cooperating native compaction returned the partial result it produced during timeout cancellation; the real native `AgentSession` path aborted compaction without preserving the timeout reason, so a result produced after timeout cancellation was still classified as plain aborted work; the timeout-abort classification originally accepted any matching error name, which left a late-settling timeout result able to append after the safety wrapper had already rejected; and the first bounded-acceptance implementation expired the token before `AgentSession`'s post-await commit gate could observe it.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** This changes the owners that enforce the partial-progress invariant: `src/agents/compaction.ts` carries partial summaries from inner chunk failures, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` preserves the timeout reason, gives the native post-await commit continuation a bounded chance to return cancellation-produced progress before the timeout rejection wins, and expires timeout-result acceptance once the wrapper rejects; `src/agents/sessions/agent-session.ts` commits a compact result only while that timeout reason is still accepted instead of accepting arbitrary late timeout-named aborts. The copy change stays at the preflight compaction failure formatter.
- **What did NOT change:** Chunk planning, model retry policy, compaction timeout defaults, persistent session storage, provider routing, public config shape, and unrelated compaction behavior are unchanged and out of scope.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `src/agents/compaction.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for summary partial-progress behavior, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` owns the native compaction wall-clock race, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for preflight agent-run failure copy.
- **Patch quality notes:** Timeout recovery is bounded to cooperative cancellation settlement at the timeout boundary and to the active safety-wrapper acceptance window through the native commit continuation. Non-timeout aborts still reject promptly, non-settling compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored by `AgentSession`, and the default 180s timeout is unchanged.
- **Target test file:** `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts`, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression test scenarios: a two-chunk compaction where chunk 1 succeeds and chunk 2 throws `TimeoutError` must return the chunk 1 summary plus a `[Partial summary: ...]` marker; a compaction canceled by outer timeout and immediately resolving with progress must return that progress instead of `Compaction timed out`; direct native compaction passes the safety timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` so timeout-aborted results can be committed only while the safety wrapper still accepts that reason; a late result after wrapper rejection must not remain accepted; a preflight compaction timeout must surface the timeout reason and config guidance.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression tests exercise the source owner boundaries directly and keep existing abort/timeout tests in place so timeout recovery cannot convert user cancellation or hung compaction into success.
- **Out of scope:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, change the shape/default of `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds`, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

## Root Cause

`src/agents/compaction.ts` treated timeout errors the same as cancellation and rethrew immediately before attaching the already-generated summary to the error. `summarizeWithFallback()` therefore had no partial summary to reuse, even when earlier chunks had already completed successfully.

The outer safety timeout added a second loss path: `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` raced native compaction against a timeout rejection. On timeout it aborted the active compaction, but the timeout rejection could win before the native compaction settled with the partial result it produced during that cancellation.

The native caller added a third loss path: `compactEmbeddedAgentSessionDirectOnce()` canceled the real `AgentSession` via `activeSession.abortCompaction()` with no timeout reason, and `AgentSession.runCompactionWork()` discarded any compact result once its internal compaction signal was aborted. Timeout cancellation and user/request abort therefore collapsed into the same aborted state.

Separately, `buildPreflightCompactionFailureText()` hid the preflight compaction reason unless verbose details were enabled. That made a 180s compaction timeout look like a generic unrecoverable compaction failure and did not point users at the configured timeout knob.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Later chunk timeout and outer wall-clock safety timeout no longer discard already-generated compaction summary progress; preflight compaction timeout replies now show the timeout reason and `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` guidance.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux OpenClaw worktrees. Original wall-clock/runtime proof was collected on `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043`, branch `feat/issue-92043`; latest conflict-resolution verification was collected on `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899`, rebased head `cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f` against `origin/main` `f9fc2efe681aa1975319c854d9dc6216e6b08935`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_REPO=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 node --import tsx /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.mjs > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.out.json
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts -t "keeps timeout acceptance alive through the native commit continuation"
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts
```

```bash
git -C /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 diff --check
```

```bash
git -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" diff --check
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "proof": "compaction-real-wallclock-timeout",
  "head": "5013dda6bd70253d7614c12257a8e7a3b0e94c03",
  "timerMode": "real setTimeout; no fake timers; no Vitest",
  "timeoutMs": 120,
  "elapsedMs": 123,
  "result": "partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout",
  "events": [
    { "event": "compaction-start", "aborted": false },
    {
      "event": "timeout-abort-observed",
      "reasonName": "CompactionTimeoutError",
      "reasonMessage": "Compaction timed out"
    },
    {
      "event": "onCancel",
      "reasonName": "CompactionTimeoutError",
      "reasonMessage": "Compaction timed out"
    },
    { "event": "partial-summary-committed-after-timeout-abort" }
  ]
}
```

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  2 passed | 48 skipped (50)
```

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  103 passed (103)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 20.81s
```

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit exited 0 with no stdout/stderr output.
```

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 21ms on 2 files using 1 threads.
```

```text
git diff --check exited 0 with no whitespace errors.
```

```text
2026-06-18 rebase verification on head cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f:
- git diff --check: passed with no output
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts
- [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 197.93s
- Test Files: 4 passed
- Tests: 109 passed
```

- **Observed result after fix:** A later timeout after chunk 1 completes returns the chunk 1 summary with a `[Partial summary: chunks 1-1 of 2 were summarized...]` marker instead of the generic unavailable summary. The real wall-clock proof ran the production `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wrapper outside Vitest/fake timers, observed a real timeout abort with `CompactionTimeoutError`, called `onCancel` with that reason, and returned `partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout`. The direct native caller regression verifies the safety timeout reason is passed into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)`, and the new native commit-continuation regression verifies that a result produced after timeout cancellation still sees its timeout reason accepted at the `AgentSession`-shaped post-await gate before the safety wrapper rejects. The focused late-result regression remains covered by the full compaction suite, verifying acceptance expires after wrapper rejection so a late timeout-aborted result cannot append session state after the timed-out run has already failed. Hung compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`; run-level aborts still reject as aborts. After rebasing onto `origin/main` `f9fc2efe681aa1975319c854d9dc6216e6b08935`, the conflict-resolved exact head `cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f` still passes the focused compaction safety, partial summary, native compact hook, and direct runtime config regression files (109 tests).
- **What was not tested:** No live external model provider, full 180s gateway compaction, Desktop session, or external channel transport was exercised in this latest rebase pass. The prior proof uses the production safety-timeout wrapper with real wall-clock timers and no Vitest/fake timers; the native `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` handoff and current conflict-resolved head remain covered by focused direct-runtime regression tests.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 [P2]: bounded timeout-aborted native compaction commit window.** This remains an intentional, narrow runtime policy for maintainer acceptance. The accepted reason is a `WeakSet` entry created by the safety wrapper's own timeout error, not any error with a matching name. Current-head regressions cover both the allowed native post-await commit continuation (`keeps timeout acceptance alive through the native commit continuation`) and expiry after the safety wrapper rejects (`expires timeout-result acceptance after the safety wrapper rejects`).
- **RF-002 [P1]: no live slow-provider/full 180-second gateway compaction.** Still disclosed. The latest rebase pass did not run a live provider or full 180s gateway scenario; current confidence comes from the prior real wall-clock safety-wrapper proof, source tracing, exact-head focused regressions, and CI-visible branch checks.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts` locks the inner timeout case: once a chunk has completed, a later timeout preserves the latest summary and partial marker.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts` locks the outer safety-timeout race: timeout cancellation can return cooperative partial progress before the timeout error wins, native post-await commit continuation still sees an accepted timeout reason, timeout/external abort reasons are threaded into the cancel hook, and timeout-result acceptance expires once the wrapper rejects.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts` locks the real direct native caller: safety timeout cancellation passes the timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` before the native compact result is returned.
- The existing abort and hung-timeout tests remain green: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into partial-success summaries, and non-settling compactions still reject with timeout.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** session-state, reliability, user-facing copy, timeout handling.
- **Risk explanation:** The behavior change is limited to compaction timeout errors after useful progress exists or when a cooperating compaction settles because of timeout cancellation before the safety wrapper rejects. First-chunk failures still fall through to the existing generic fallback, hung compactions still time out, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored, and non-timeout aborts still propagate as cancellation.
- **Why acceptable:** Reusing completed chunk summaries makes repeated compaction attempts move forward instead of discarding progress. The copy change only reveals timeout reasons for compaction timeout failures; generic non-verbose compaction errors remain redacted.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

target.title: feat(agents): add sessions_delegate and sessions_delegate_batch tools
target.body:
## Summary
- Problem: OpenClaw agents can spawn background sessions (`sessions_spawn`) but cannot synchronously delegate a task and receive structured output back as a tool result. Complex parallelizable work runs sequentially or requires manual user coordination.

- Why it matters: Multi-agent orchestration (fan-out/fan-in, map-reduce, pipeline patterns) is blocked without a synchronous delegation primitive.

- What changed:

  - Added `sessions_delegate` tool — blocking spawn-and-wait that returns child output as a tool result

  - Added `sessions_delegate_batch` tool — parallel fan-out of multiple delegate tasks with configurable failure mode

  - Wired up the existing but unused `prepareSubagentSpawn` context engine hook in `subagent-spawn.ts`

  - Registered both tools in tool catalog, display config, and openclaw-tools

- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `sessions_spawn` behavior is untouched. No changes to auto-announce, session lanes, or existing lifecycle hooks. No plugin-layer orchestration patterns yet (map-reduce, pipeline, race) — this PR provides the runtime primitives only.



## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra


## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Two new agent tools available: `sessions_delegate` and `sessions_delegate_batch`
- `sessions_delegate`: spawns a child agent, blocks until completion, returns output directly as tool result (default timeout 120s, default cleanup "delete")
- `sessions_delegate_batch`: fans out 1–10 tasks in parallel, returns collected results with per-task status and summary. `failureMode: "partial"` (default) returns completed results even if some fail; `"all"` fails the batch if any task fails.


## Diagram (if applicable)

```text

**Before (sessions_spawn):**

[parent calls sessions_spawn] -> [child runs async] -> [auto-announce as user message] -> [parent resumes on next turn]

**After (sessions_delegate):**

[parent calls sessions_delegate] -> [child runs] -> [tool blocks via agent.wait] -> [reads frozenResultText] -> [returns as tool result in same turn]

**After (sessions_delegate_batch):**

[parent calls sessions_delegate_batch] -> [N children spawn] -> [Promise.allSettled] -> [collect results] -> [return aggregated tool result]

```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `Yes` — two new agent tools, but they reuse existing `spawnSubagentDirect` which enforces depth limits, active children limits, sandbox policies, and visibility guards.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — reuses existing `agent.wait` gateway RPC
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — new tools registered in catalog
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Risk + mitigation: New tools inherit all existing spawn guards (max depth, max active children, sandbox mode, session lane isolation). No new escalation paths.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

target.title: docs: add Plugin Inspector development and CI workflow
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a canonical Plugin Inspector guide for plugin-author development, CI, trusted runtime capture, beta-checkout comparison, reports, and plugin-shipped skill proof.
- Reorder the plugin testing guide so external authors see package, runtime, and live-test guidance before OpenClaw's repo-only test helper reference.
- Link the workflow from the plugin quickstart and tool-plugin CI guide, and replace the stale future-tense Inspector section in the compatibility reference.
- Keep the full Plugin Inspector command/API reference in its owner repository; this PR documents how it fits into OpenClaw plugin development.

## Linked context

Related: https://github.com/openclaw/clawhub/pull/2627

Requested during the ClawHub/OpenClaw plugin documentation consolidation work.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw's canonical docs did not explain how external plugin authors should use Plugin Inspector during development and CI, while the testing page led with repo-only helpers and the compatibility page described the Inspector as future work.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw docs checkout at `2ab6caeb80` and the published `@openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.3.15` CLI.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx --yes @openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.3.15 --help`; `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm format:docs:check`; `pnpm docs:check-mdx`; `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` with the new page included; `pnpm lint:docs`; `pnpm docs:check-links`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): `pnpm docs:list` lists `plugins/plugin-inspector.md`; the current Inspector CLI help confirms the documented `check`, `inspect`, `ci`, `config`, `init`, checkout, runtime, author-facing, SARIF, and JUnit surfaces; all listed repository docs checks pass.
- Observed result after fix: the new page is discoverable in navigation and passes OpenClaw's formatting, MDX, glossary, lint, JSON, and internal-link checks.
- What was not tested: a completed local Mintlify render and Mintlify anchor audit.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the local Mintlify preview and `mint broken-links --check-anchors` stop on existing parse errors in unrelated current-main pages under `docs/plugins/reference/`; the pnpm anchor runner also hits an isolated Mintlify `react` peer-resolution error.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): `docs/plugins/compatibility.md` used a future-tense day-one CLI and stale `@openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.1.0` examples; `docs/plugins/sdk-testing.md` started with repo-only bundled-plugin helpers.

- `pnpm format:docs:check`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` with the new page included through a temporary index
- `pnpm lint:docs`
- `pnpm docs:check-links`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD`

No runtime test was added because this is a docs-only change.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`, documentation only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`.

What is the highest-risk area? Inaccurate or unsafe Plugin Inspector command guidance.

How is that risk mitigated? The workflow is grounded in the current published Inspector README, package metadata, CLI help, and CLI source. Runtime capture is explicitly separated and limited to trusted code.

Ready for maintainer review and CI. No known author-side changes remain.

target.title: fix(telegram): respect group-level groupPolicy: open for /models callbacks in forum topics
target.body:
## Problem

In Telegram forum topics, clicking provider/model buttons from the `/models` inline menu silently does nothing when:
- the account-level `channels.telegram.groupPolicy` is `"allowlist"`,
- `channels.telegram.groupAllowFrom` is empty, and
- the specific group is configured with `groupPolicy: "open"` under `channels.telegram.groups.<chatId>`.

Native commands work in that same chat, but the model callback path drops the update without any visible error.

## Root cause

`isTelegramModelCallbackAuthorized` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts` only evaluated the account-level allowlist via `resolveTelegramCommandIngressAuthorization`. It never consulted the per-group `groupPolicy` override, so a group explicitly marked `"open"` was still rejected because the global `groupAllowFrom` was empty.

## Fix

Add a group policy evaluation step before the existing fallback, mirroring what native commands already do:
- Pass `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: true` so `groupConfig.groupPolicy` is honored.
- Pass `allowEmptyAllowlistEntries: true` and `checkChatAllowlist: true` so a group listed in `groups` is treated as explicitly allowed even when the global allowlist is empty.
- Short-circuit with `true` when the effective policy is `"open"`.
- If the policy evaluation rejects, log the reason and return `false`.

## Regression test

Added `extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.forum-models.test.ts` (collected `*.test.ts` filename) with two forum-topic cases:
- `mdl_list_openai_1` verifies the original message is edited into the provider's model list.
- `mdl_sel_openai/gpt-5.4` verifies the model selection callback updates the message with the success text.

## Verification

Full telegram extension suite:

```
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/

Test Files  120 passed (120)
     Tests  2072 passed (2072)
Duration  87.94s
```

Focused regression test:

```
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.forum-models.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  2 passed (2)
```

## Real Telegram proof

See PR comment below for terminal-based proof that `/models` provider/model buttons update the message after the patch.

Fixes #92655
